lectureTitle: Digital Identities and IAM (Identity and Access Management)

examQuestions.promptVerbatim: What is a digital identity? Discuss important concepts, terms and principles.
Explain the difference between identification, identity proofing and authentication.
Explain the relationship between claims, assertions, and credentials, and discuss the key technologies that are used in these.

drills.question: When users act online they cannot bring their physical self. What is a digital identity, and why is it needed?
keyBeats: Online: cannot present physical self
Digital identity = package of info representing user
Set of attributes / claims about a subject
Used to access protected resources, personalize services
drills.modelAnswer: Online a user has no physical body to present, so they are represented by a digital identity, which can be viewed as a package of information about themselves. It is a set of characteristics (attributes) representing a person or entity, used to gain access to protected resources and content and to let services be personalized. Cameron defines it more formally as a set of claims made by one digital subject about itself or another.
drills.citation: When we, as users, are acting online, we are represented by a digital identity — Lecture-2.txt, PAGE 11

drills.question: Defining who exists digitally requires precise terms. Define digital subject, attribute, partial identity, and complete identity.
keyBeats: Digital subject = person/thing in digital realm
Attribute = type + value (Department / Engineering)
Partial identity = subset of attributes
Complete identity = union of all attributes
drills.modelAnswer: A digital subject is a person or thing represented or existing in the digital realm which is being described or dealt with. A personal attribute is a distinct and measurable property of a subject, described by an attribute type (e.g., first name) and an attribute value (e.g., John). A partial identity is a subset of attributes used to characterize but not necessarily identify a person, while a complete digital identity is the union of all the attributes.
drills.citation: Complete (digital) identity: The union of all the attributes — Lecture-2.txt, PAGE 15

drills.question: Privacy depends on whether two partial identities can be tied together. What is linkability, and how does unlinkability protect privacy?
keyBeats: Linkability = others can tell if partial identities relate
Unlinkability = no one can distinguish if sets relate
Unlinkability protects privacy
Useful when real identity not needed
drills.modelAnswer: Linkability is the possibility for others to distinguish sufficiently whether given sets of attributes (partial identities) are related or not. Conversely, two sets of data are unlinkable if no one else can distinguish whether these sets of data are related or not. Unlinkability supports privacy protection and matters when the real identity is not needed, since it stops separate partial identities being correlated into a fuller profile.
drills.citation: The possibility for others to distinguish sufficiently whether given sets of attributes — Lecture-2.txt, PAGE 18

drills.question: The course distinguishes legal vs technical privacy concepts. What is Personal Data (GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation)) versus PII (Personally Identifiable Information) (NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology))?
keyBeats: Personal data (GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation)) = any info relating to identifiable natural person
Identifiers: name, ID number, location, online identifier
PII (Personally Identifiable Information) (NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology)) = info to distinguish/trace an individual
Privacy = control over collection/use/disclosure, not secrecy
drills.modelAnswer: Under EU GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation), personal data is any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person, who can be identified directly or indirectly by an identifier such as a name, ID number, location data or online identifier. NIST's (National Institute of Standards and Technology's) PII (Personally Identifiable Information) is any information that can be used to distinguish or trace an individual's identity, plus any other information linked or linkable to that individual. Privacy itself is the claim or right of individuals to exercise control over the collection, use and disclosure of their personal information; it is about control, not secrecy.
drills.citation: Any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person — Lecture-2.txt, PAGE 36

drills.question: Cameron argued identity systems fail without guiding principles. Name his 7 Laws of Identity and state what the first two require.
keyBeats: Cameron: identity systems fail without guiding principles
The 7 Laws, each at one-line depth
Together they define a user-centric identity metasystem
drills.modelAnswer: Kim Cameron argued identity systems fail without guiding principles, and set out seven Laws of Identity: 1) User Control & Consent — only reveal identifying information with the user's consent; 2) Minimal Disclosure for a Constrained Use — disclose the least identifying information and limit its use; 3) Justifiable Parties — disclosure is limited to parties having a necessary and justifiable place in the relationship; 4) Directed Identity — support omni-directional identifiers for public entities and uni-directional ones for private entities, preventing unnecessary correlation handles; 5) Pluralism of Operators & Technologies — channel and enable the interworking of multiple identity technologies run by multiple providers; 6) Human Integration — make the human user a reliable component of the system (defend the human-machine channel against attacks like phishing); 7) Consistent Experience Across Contexts — guarantee a simple, consistent user experience while enabling separation of contexts. Together these define a user-centric, privacy-respecting identity metasystem.
drills.citation: Technical identity systems must only reveal information identifying a user with the user's consent — TheLawsOfIdentity.txt, PAGE 06

drills.question: Christopher Allen traced identity toward user autonomy. Sketch his four evolution stages and a few of his 10 SSI (Self-Sovereign Identity) principles.
keyBeats: Four stages: centralized, federated, user-centric, self-sovereign
SSI (Self-Sovereign Identity) = users are rulers of their own identity
Principles incl. Existence, Control, Consent, Minimalization
Compare to GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) data minimization and Cameron's laws
drills.modelAnswer: Allen describes online identity advancing through four stages: centralized, federated, user-centric, and self-sovereign identity, where the user moves from being locked to one authority to being the ruler of their own identity. His ten principles include Existence, Control, Access, Transparency, Persistence, Portability, Interoperability, Consent, Minimalization and Protection. These overlap with Cameron's laws and GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) ideas such as consent and data minimization (e.g., disclosing a minimum age rather than the exact date of birth).
drills.citation: centralized identity, federated identity, user-centric identity, and self-sovereign identity — Path to SSI - Alacrity.txt, line 51

drills.question: These three terms are often confused. What is identification, and what makes it distinct from the other two?
keyBeats: Identification = process by an observer in a context
Selecting one and only one entity
About uniqueness among a group
Done at a point in time, not the same as proofing/auth
drills.modelAnswer: Identification is a process done by an observer, and it is always done in a context. It is about uniqueness: selecting one and only one entity with certain characteristics among a group of entities in a given context. Whether a given attribute identifies someone therefore depends on the context (one John in Engineering identifies, many Johns in the EU do not).
drills.citation: Identification is a process done by an observer, and identification is done in a context — Lecture-2.txt, PAGE 17

drills.question: KYC (Know Your Customer) services need to know the real person behind an account. What is identity proofing, and how does it differ from identification?
keyBeats: Identity proofing = establish link to real-life person
To some degree of certainty/assurance
NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) 800-63A; enrollment, ID evidence submitted
Differs: proofing binds account to real human, once at enrollment
drills.modelAnswer: Identity proofing is the process of establishing, to some degree of certainty or assurance, a relationship between a subject accessing online services and a real-life person, e.g. for Know Your Customer services like banking. It happens during enrollment, when the applicant submits ID evidence and attributes to a CSP (Credential Service Provider). Unlike plain identification (singling out one entity), proofing binds the digital identity to a verified real-world individual, measured by Identity Assurance Levels IAL1 (Identity Assurance Level 1)-IAL3 (Identity Assurance Level 3).
drills.citation: the process of establishing, to some degree of certainty or assurance, a relationship — Lecture-2.txt, PAGE 23

drills.question: Assurance levels grade how strong the binding to a real person is. Summarize NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) IALs (Identity Assurance Levels) and eIDAS (Electronic Identification, Authentication and Trust Services) LOAs (Levels of Assurance).
keyBeats: IAL1 (Identity Assurance Level 1) self-asserted; IAL2 (Identity Assurance Level 2) remote/in-person; IAL3 (Identity Assurance Level 3) in-person physical docs
eIDAS (Electronic Identification, Authentication and Trust Services) LOA (Level of Assurance): Low, Substantial, High
Higher level = more confidence in claimed identity
Level chosen by sensitivity of information
drills.modelAnswer: NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) defines three Identity Assurance Levels: at IAL1 (Identity Assurance Level 1) attributes are self-asserted, IAL2 (Identity Assurance Level 2) requires remote or in-person identity proofing, and IAL3 (Identity Assurance Level 3) requires in-person proofing with physical documentation checked by a CSP (Credential Service Provider) representative. eIDAS (Electronic Identification, Authentication and Trust Services) uses three Levels of Assurance, Low, Substantial and High, giving increasing degrees of confidence in the claimed identity. Service providers pick a level depending on the sensitivity of the information they manage.
drills.citation: At IAL3 (Identity Assurance Level 3), in-person identity proofing is required — Lecture-2.txt, PAGE 23

drills.question: Authentication is the third concept. How does it differ from identification, and why might you authenticate without identifying?
keyBeats: Authentication = presentation vs proof of ownership
Can authenticate without identifying the real person
Identify when payment involved; authenticate to grant access
Shift away from user-IDs/passwords toward passkeys, key-pairs
drills.modelAnswer: Authentication is about presenting or proving ownership of a credential to be granted access, and it does not necessarily identify you. The course stresses you can authenticate a user without identifying the real person, whereas you would identify someone (e.g., when payment is involved) only when the real identity is needed. The course notes a shift away from user-IDs and passwords toward passkeys, verification and extensive use of public-private key-pairs.
drills.citation: Authenticate you — not necessarily identifying you! — Lecture-2.txt, PAGE 53

drills.question: Put the three in order over an identity lifecycle. How do proofing, identification and authentication relate to authorization?
keyBeats: Proofing at enrollment binds account to real person
Identification = singling out one entity in context
Authentication = proving you hold the credential later
Authorization = what you are allowed to do
drills.modelAnswer: Identity proofing happens once at enrollment, binding a credential to a verified real-life person. Identification is the act of singling out one and only one entity in a context, while authentication is later proving you own/hold that credential when you return. Authorization is separate again and answers what you are allowed to do once authenticated. The lecture frames these together as Identification, Authentication and Authorization in security.
drills.citation: Understanding Identification, Authentication & Authorization in Security — Lecture-2.txt, PAGE 30

drills.question: On the Internet nobody can vouch for you in person. What is a claim, and why did Cameron say identity should be based on claims?
keyBeats: Internet built without way to know who you connect to
Claim = assertion of truth of something, disputed/in doubt
Claim = set of claims by one subject about another
Claims separate presentation from provable link to real world
drills.modelAnswer: The Internet was built without a way to know who and what you are connecting to, so Cameron proposed basing identity on claims. A claim is an assertion of the truth of something, typically one which is disputed or in doubt, and a digital identity is a set of claims made by one digital subject about itself or another digital subject. The advantage is that the presentation of claims is separated from the provability of the link to a real-world object, letting trust be handled at a higher layer.
drills.citation: an assertion of the truth of something, typically one which is disputed or in doubt — TheLawsOfIdentity.txt, PAGE 05

drills.question: Claims alone may not be believed. What is an assertion, and how does it relate to a claim?
keyBeats: Assertion = confident, forceful statement of fact or belief
Someone asserts to add credibility to a claim
Not a guaranteed proof; always subject to doubt
Relying party must still evaluate
drills.modelAnswer: An assertion is a confident and forceful statement of fact or belief; somebody can make an assertion to add credibility to the claim presented. It is not a guaranteed proof and assertions need always be subject to doubt, but it may be better than nothing. The relying party still has to evaluate the claim and decide whether to trust it, considering who made the assertion and whether they are trustworthy.
drills.citation: A confident and forceful statement of fact or belief — Lecture-2.txt, PAGE 26

drills.question: Credentials package claims for reuse. What is a credential, and who issues it?
keyBeats: Credential = collection of info attesting to stated facts
Paper (passport) or token (smartcard)
Certified container of attributes issued by an Issuer
Issuer = trusted 3rd party; may be self-issued
drills.modelAnswer: A credential is a generic term covering both paper documents like passports and non-paper objects like smartcards; it is a collection of information attached to an entity and attesting to the integrity of certain stated facts, i.e. a certified container of attributes of a user issued by an Issuer. Credentials are usually issued by a trusted third party who vouches for the correctness of the contained information, though the issuer may also be the user (self-issued credentials).
drills.citation: a certified container of attributes of a User issued by an Issuer — Lecture-2.txt, PAGE 45

drills.question: There are several ways to present a claim with differing trust. What are the three ways shown, and how do they differ?
keyBeats: Self-issued (unverified) claim
Claim with assertion from trusted IdP (Identity Provider)
SSI (Self-Sovereign Identity): Verifiable Presentation from Verifiable Credentials
VP (Verifiable Presentation) can be verified against the Issuer
drills.modelAnswer: The lecture shows three ways to present claims: self-issued and unverified; a claim accompanied by an assertion from a trusted Identity Provider; and self-sovereign identity using Verifiable Credentials from trusted issuers, presented as a Verifiable Presentation. They differ in trust: a self-issued claim carries no third-party backing, an IdP (Identity Provider) assertion adds credibility, and a Verifiable Presentation can be verified cryptographically against the Issuer.
drills.citation: Self-sovereign identity Using Verifiable Credentials from trusted issuers — Lecture-2.txt, PAGE 29

drills.question: JWTs (JSON Web Tokens) are a concrete carrier of claims. How does a JWT embody the claim/assertion idea?
keyBeats: JWT (JSON Web Token) structure: header, payload, signature
Payload carries the claims
Signature (HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code)-SHA256) = the assertion
Cameron's claims model in practice
drills.modelAnswer: A JSON Web Token is given as the example technology for claims and assertions. The token's payload carries the claims about the subject, and the signature, an HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code)-SHA256 over the encoded header and payload, acts as the assertion that backs those claims. This realizes Cameron's claims-based model: attributes are expressed as claims and a signature provides the credibility-adding assertion.
drills.citation: Example: Using a JSON Web Token (JWT) — Lecture-2.txt, PAGE 26

drills.question: Many standards bodies define these technologies. Which bodies own the key claim/credential standards mentioned?
keyBeats: W3C (World Wide Web Consortium): Verifiable Credentials, Decentralized Identifiers, WebAuthN
IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force): OAuth (Open Authorization) 2.0, JSON Web Tokens
OASIS: SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language), XACML (Extensible Access Control Markup Language)
FIDO (Fast IDentity Online) Alliance: passkeys, CTAP (Client to Authenticator Protocol), WebAuthN
drills.modelAnswer: Several standardization bodies own the technologies behind claims, assertions and credentials. The W3C (World Wide Web Consortium) defines Verifiable Credentials, Decentralized Identifiers and Web Authentication; the IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force) defines OAuth (Open Authorization) 2.0 and JSON Web Tokens; OASIS defines the Security Assertion Markup Language (SAML) and XACML (Extensible Access Control Markup Language); and the FIDO (Fast IDentity Online) Alliance works on passkeys, CTAP (Client to Authenticator Protocol) and WebAuthN for stronger authentication.
drills.citation: World Wide Web Consortium (W3C): ... Verifiable Credentials. Decentralized Identifiers — Lecture-2.txt, PAGE 32

drills.question: Credentials get reused beyond their purpose. Distinguish primary and secondary use of credentials.
keyBeats: Primary use = intended purpose (passport to cross border)
Secondary use = other purposes than originally intended
Raises regulatory/technological concerns
Concern applies to online credentials too
drills.modelAnswer: Primary use means using credentials for their intended purpose, such as a passport for crossing borders or a driver's license shown to a police officer. Secondary use is making use of such information for other purposes than originally intended by the issuer, whether legitimate or not. Secondary use raises technological and regulatory concerns about how to collect, store, aggregate, link and transmit personal information properly, and is also a concern for online credentials.
drills.citation: Making use of such information for other purposes than originally intended — Lecture-2.txt, PAGE 48

exercises.promptVerbatim: What is a digital identity? Give definitions and explain how digital identities are used
exercises.whatItTests: Definition of digital identity and its purpose/use online.
exercises.answerSketch: Package of information / set of attributes representing a user online; Cameron: set of claims by one digital subject about another. Used to access protected resources and personalize services, can act as an online credential.

exercises.promptVerbatim: Explain important concepts and terms related to digital identities: Digital subject, identifier, attributes, claim, assertion, partial identity
exercises.answerSketch: Digital subject = person/thing in digital realm; identifier = info characterizing exactly one entity in a context; attribute = type+value property; claim = assertion of truth in doubt; assertion = forceful statement adding credibility; partial identity = subset of attributes.

exercises.promptVerbatim: Why is it a good idea to base identities on claims?
exercises.answerSketch: Separates presentation of claims from provability of link to real world; trust/attribution/usefulness factored out to a higher layer; encompasses all identity systems; allows non-unique identifiers and a metasystem.

exercises.promptVerbatim: What is identification? Give examples of digital identities that you can use without being identified! Explain what linkability means
exercises.answerSketch: Identification = observer selecting one and only one entity in a context (uniqueness). Examples usable without identifying: browsing self-asserted identity, nickname, one who knows a password, owner of a passkey. Linkability = possibility for others to tell whether partial identities are related.

exercises.promptVerbatim: What is Personal Data and Personally Identifiable Information (PII)?
exercises.whatItTests: GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) personal data vs NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) PII (Personally Identifiable Information) definitions.
exercises.answerSketch: Personal data (GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation)) = any info relating to identified/identifiable natural person via identifiers (name, ID number, location, online identifier). PII (Personally Identifiable Information) (NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) 800-122) = info to distinguish/trace an individual plus any info linked or linkable to them.

exercises.promptVerbatim: Discuss the “Laws of identity” and Christopher Allen’s “10 principles of self-sovereign identity” and compare with GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation)!
exercises.whatItTests: Cameron's 7 laws, Allen's 10 SSI (Self-Sovereign Identity) principles, GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) alignment.
exercises.answerSketch: Cameron 7 laws (control/consent, minimal disclosure, justifiable parties, directed identity, pluralism, human integration, consistent experience). Allen 10 SSI (Self-Sovereign Identity) principles (existence, control, access, transparency, persistence, portability, interoperability, consent, minimalization, protection). Compare: consent, data minimization, purpose limitation echo GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) (Art. 23/30). The 7 Laws (one-line each): 1) User Control & Consent — only reveal identifying information with the user's consent; 2) Minimal Disclosure for a Constrained Use — disclose the least identifying information and limit its use; 3) Justifiable Parties — disclosure is limited to parties having a necessary and justifiable place in the relationship; 4) Directed Identity — support omni-directional identifiers for public entities and uni-directional ones for private entities, preventing unnecessary correlation handles; 5) Pluralism of Operators & Technologies — channel and enable the interworking of multiple identity technologies run by multiple providers; 6) Human Integration — make the human user a reliable component of the system (defend the human-machine channel against attacks like phishing); 7) Consistent Experience Across Contexts — guarantee a simple, consistent user experience while enabling separation of contexts.

exercises.promptVerbatim: What can be done to minimize disclosure of information?
exercises.answerSketch: Need-to-know / need-to-retain; least identifying information; selective disclosure; range proofs and zero-knowledge techniques; avoid cross-context unique identifiers; privacy by design.

exercises.promptVerbatim: Discuss the differences between physical and online credentials! What do we mean by primary and secondary use of credentials? Is secondary use also a problem for online credentials?
exercises.whatItTests: Physical vs online credentials; primary/secondary use.
exercises.answerSketch: Physical = paper/token issued by state/orgs (passport, CPR (Civil Population Register) card), can be faked/stolen, harder to verify remotely; online = digital, verifiable cryptographically (e.g. Verifiable Credentials). Primary use = intended purpose; secondary use = other purposes than intended, raising aggregation/linking concerns. Yes, secondary use also affects online credentials.
